title: The Swarm push cycle serializes, AES-256-GCM encrypts, uploads to /bytes, then writes a feed SOC.
content:
## Claim
On every new ordinal beyond the outbox cursor, SwarmChannel pushes operations via: serialize → encrypt → /bytes upload → feed write → manifest update → cursor advance.

## Step-by-step
1. SyncManager detects new ordinals in `operation_index_operations` beyond the outbox cursor.
2. Ops are grouped by `(documentId, scope, branch)` into `SyncOperation` objects.
3. Each `SyncOperation` is added to `SwarmChannel.outbox`.
4. `outbox.onAdded` fires, running:
   - serialize to JSON
   - encrypt with AES-256-GCM (wallet-derived key)
   - upload to `/bytes` endpoint on the Bee node
   - write the reference to the document's feed (SOC)
   - update the drive manifest feed
   - call `syncOp.executed()` — SyncManager advances the cursor in `sync_cursors`.

## Why this ordering matters
The cursor only advances after the feed write succeeds, so a mid-push failure leaves the outbox ready to retry the same batch on next cycle — no data loss, no duplicates.

## Source
Swarm Channel Architecture, "Push Cycle (local → Swarm)"; `adapter/src/channel/swarm-channel.ts`.
description: SyncManager groups new ops into SyncOperation batches, SwarmChannel encrypts, uploads to the Bee /bytes endpoint, writes a feed SOC, and advances the outbox cursor.
